message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about a design doc (the doc is at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt; the repo is /home/jleechan/projects/worktree_runner; you may use WebSearch/WebFetch and read files). Try to REFUTE it. Finding from the "rust" reviewer, severity critical:
TITLE: serde_yaml is deprecated/archived — do not build a new tool on it
DETAIL: Cargo.toml (gistfile1.txt line 91) declares `serde_yaml = "0.9"`. dtolnay archived the serde_yaml repo in March 2024; the final release is 0.9.34+deprecated and it receives no fixes. A brand-new v1 tool whose core job is generating/validating YAML config should not start life on an archived parser. Worse, the skeleton never actually calls serde_yaml — see the next finding — so right now it is a dead dependency on an unmaintained crate.
RECOMMENDATION: Pick a maintained YAML path before any code lands: `serde_yaml_ng` or `serde_norway` (maintained forks with the same API), or sidestep YAML entirely if gha-outrunner accepts JSON (`serde_json` is first-party maintained). Decide once in v1; migrating serializers later touches every config test.
Is the finding factually correct and does the recommendation make sense? If the factual basis is wrong or the severity is inflated, refute it. Default to refuted=true only if you have concrete contrary evidence or the claim is unsupported after checking.

input.description: Find serde references in design doc
Find all YAML references in doc

input.command: curl -s --max-time 15 "https://crates.io/api/v1/crates/serde_yaml" -H "User-Agent: fact-check-script" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); c=d['crate']; print('max_version:', c['max_version']); print('updated_at:', c['updated_at']); print('description:', c.get('description'))"
input.description: Check serde_yaml status on crates.io API

input.command: curl -s --max-time 15 "https://api.github.com/repos/dtolnay/serde-yaml" -H "User-Agent: fact-check-script" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print('archived:', d.get('archived')); print('pushed_at:', d.get('pushed_at')); print('full_name:', d.get('full_name'))"
input.description: Check if dtolnay/serde-yaml repo is archived

content.content:
max_version: 0.9.34+deprecated
updated_at: 2024-03-25T00:50:19.759577Z
description: YAML data format for Serde

content.text: Both external facts confirmed. Now let me verify the recommended alternative crates exist and are maintained.

input.command: for c in serde_yaml_ng serde_norway; do curl -s --max-time 15 "https://crates.io/api/v1/crates/$c" -H "User-Agent: fact-check-script" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); c=d.get('crate',{}); print(c.get('id'), '| max:', c.get('max_version'), '| updated:', c.get('updated_at'), '| downloads:', c.get('downloads'))"; done
input.description: Check serde_yaml_ng and serde_norway on crates.io

content.content:
serde_yaml_ng | max: 0.10.0 | updated: 2024-05-26T19:57:45.710414Z | downloads: 5693900
serde_norway | max: 0.9.42 | updated: 2024-12-21T04:28:45.739429Z | downloads: 7588484

input.reasoning: All factual claims verified against primary sources. (1) The design doc's Cargo.toml at line 91 of gistfile1.txt declares serde_yaml = "0.9". (2) GitHub API confirms dtolnay/serde-yaml is archived (archived: true, last push 2024-03-25). (3) crates.io API confirms the final release is 0.9.34+deprecated (2024-03-25), matching the finding exactly. (4) The dead-dependency claim is correct: serde_yaml appears only in the Cargo.toml; the skeleton's src/config.rs generates outrunner.yml via format!/raw string literals + std::fs::write and never imports serde_yaml. (5) The recommended alternatives are real and maintained: serde_yaml_ng (0.10.0, ~5.7M downloads) and serde_norway (0.9.42, updated Dec 2024, ~7.6M downloads) both exist on crates.io as API-compatible forks; serde_json is first-party maintained. Severity is not inflated for a design-stage review: the dependency sits on the tool's core responsibility (YAML config generation/validation), and choosing the serialization path before v1 code lands is exactly when the decision is cheapest. The only nuance is that 0.9.34+deprecated still compiles and works today — the risk is unmaintained-dependency/supply-chain, not immediate breakage — but the finding claims only that it "receives no fixes," which is accurate. The recommendation is sound and actionable.
